Bot Interactions

Welcome to the tutorial on Bot Interactions!

We will take you through the basics of how to program bots to do things

(Don't worry, the "programming" will be super simple)

Saying Hi

Each of these bots is programmed to say something when you click on them.

There is a little "Edit" button to let you change the bots' @onClick tag.

There are a few things you should notice:

  1. The @onClick tag starts with an "@"
    • This means the bot is listening for something to happen
    • In this case, the "onClick" tag is listening for you to click on the bot
    • Bots can listen to other things, but we'll get to that later
  2. There are some things you can tell the "os" to do.
    • You need a period between "os" and what you want it to do
    • To make it happen, you need "()" after the command
    • In the "()" you can have words, but they need to be inside quotation marks

More Than Clicks

These bots are listening for different "events."

There is a huge list of events bots can listen for in the CasualOS Docs

Notice how both of these bots change their tags? You can change any tag by typing tags followed by a period, and setting it equal to (=) the value you want it to have.

Look out, though! You probably need quotation marks around the value you want to set. Otherwise, the computer won't know the difference between something like tags (the things bots have) or "tags" (the word).

The Orange Bot

The orange bot is listening for 4 different events! Each does something slightly different

@onPointerEnter activates when your mouse cursor goes over the bot. @onPointerExit triggers when your mouse cursor leaves the bot. Together, they can change the bot's #color to "skyblue" when your mouse is over it and back to "orange" when your mouse leaves.

@onPointerDown and @onPointerUp activate when you press down or up on the bot with your mouse. These are programmed to change the bot's #scaleZ when you click on it.

Note: we don't need to use quotation marks ("") when we're setting the scaleZ tag because computers always understand numbers.

The Blue Bot

The blue bot is only listening for 1 event, but can do 4 things! (Move Up, Down, Left, and Right)

This is because it is programmed to check which keys were pressed and react accordingly

When the bot hears the @onKeyDown event (that), it looks at that's keys and checks if any of the arrow keys are included. If any arrow keys are included, the bot sets its homeX or homeY tag to be 1 more or less than it was.

Challenge: Say Hi

Click the bot that says "@onClick" to change what happens when you click on the pink bot.

If you remember from earlier, there are a few ways bots can say things.

Here are a few ways we learned:

  • Toast
  • Tip
  • Show HTML (advanced)

If you get stuck, you can click below for a hint or for a solution.

Hint

If you want to "tip" or "toast," those are things the "os" can do. You can use those functions by typing "os.tip" or "os.toast"

Reminder: if you want a function to execute, it needs to be followed by ().

Reminder: if you want to type a word or phrase, but don't want CasualOS to interpret what it might mean, wrap it in "quotation marks".

Solution

There are a few ways you could make the bot say "Hello." Here is one way:

os.tip("Hello")

Challenge: Change Color

You should make the bot change color when you click on it.

Reminder: the bot's color is stored in its "tags"

Hint

Remember that you can access a bot's tags by typing "tags" followed by a period (.), followed by the tag you want.

You can also set the tag equal to something with the equals sign (=).

Solution

Here is a simple way to change the bot's color to blue:

tags.color = "blue"

Bonus Solution

To switch between two colors, you need some way to check which color the bot is.

There are a few ways you could do this. Here's one:

if (tags.color == "green") {
  tags.color = "blue"
} else {
  tags.color = "green"
}

If you want to learn more about "if statements," click here

Challenge: Show Input

When you click on this bot, it shows an input box you can type in. It saves what you type as a "variable."

A variable is a way to save information (like a word or phrase) that you can use later.

There are also some "comments" in the code to explain what it does. Lines that start with two slashes will be ignored.

Hint

The bot's label is stored in its tags.

You can change the bot's label just like you have changed other tags.

In this case, you just need to use what you typed in the input box instead of a word you type in quotation marks (").

Solution

The simplest way to change the bot's label is to put this on line 6:

tags.label = newLabel

Since there are no quotes around newLabel, CasualOS will use what it was set equal to earlier.

Challenge: Move

You should make the bot move when you click on it.

Reminder: the bot's position is stored in its "tags" as #homeX, #homeY, and #homeZ

Hint

You can move the bot anywhere you want.

You just need to change at least one of its position tags.

This bot starts at homeX = -3, homeY = 1, homeZ = 0

Solution

Here is a really simple way to change the bot's position:

tags.homeX = -2

Bonus Solution

To change the position every time the bot is clicked, we can check where it is and add to or subtract from that

There are a few ways you could do this. Here's one:

tags.homeX = tags.homeX + 1

That will set the homeX tag equal to 1 more than it already is. We actually have a shorter way to write this, though:

tags.homeX += 1

Either way will work, the second way is just a shortcut to do the same thing.

Challenge: Animate

When you change a bot's tags, you can animate the change.

There are a BUNCH of options for how you animate them. You can read more in the documentation

Did you notice the "await" keyword before the "animateTag" function? This makes sure your code waits until the animation is finished before moving on.

Hint

The bot is already animated to go up. You just need another animation after that.

You just need to change where it's coming from and where it's going to.

Solution

You just need to copy & paste the existing animation, and then change its fromValue and toValue:

await animateTag(thisBot, "homeZ", {
    fromValue: 0,
    toValue: 5,
    duration: 1
})
await animateTag(thisBot, "homeZ", {
    fromValue: 5,
    toValue: 0,
    duration: 1
})
